notes: Same old Ficus, turning 15 (and after almost 12 years of training). Here it was just brought indoors for overwintering, coming from a season spent in an outdoor greenhouse, happy and lively. It almost died the following winter. I managed to save it, but the tree lost most of its finer structural branching. I had to redefine it completely starting in 2025. To be continued...
alt: Multi-trunk weeping fig in full leaf in a green oval pot topped with gravel, on a dark wooden display stand

notes: The old Ficus after a full defoliation and pruning, first day after moving to a new (and hopefully final) home. Sorry 'bout the strong edits, it was an ad-hoc shot and I had to digitally remove quite a few things from the background.
alt: Black-and-white photo of the weeping fig fully defoliated, its bare multi-trunk silhouette rising from a round pot set on a perforated brick

style: (No longer in collection) Informal upright, twin-trunk
notes: Never had much potential, but could have become a nice, desert-inspired shohin. It rather held sentimental value, as it's been with me for 9 years, having been grown from a very small leaf cutting as an experiment. Lost it in 2026 to either leaf burning, root rot or both. Guess I just don't have any luck with Crassulas.
alt: Twin-trunk finger jade with tubular green leaves in a rough bark-textured pot, staged on a red ikebana book with two cherries and a toy car

notes: Overwintering indoors while I was living in a small apartment. The entry-level UV grow lights made quite a positive difference I was not really expecting. The tree stopped yellowing and dropping the basal leaves and actively grew during the darkest winter days. Some glass vials filled with water can be observed here, directing the aerial roots towards the top of the soil and providing extra moisture.
alt: Weeping fig on a dresser under the pink-purple glow of two clamp-on LED grow lights

notes: Slightly repositioned as a tree on the edge of a desert cliff after getting it a fresh new arid-inspired, crackled pot, courtesy of Sergey Bogopolski (Сергей Богопольский), Chernivtsi.
alt: Pygmy porkbush leaning from a rugged round pot dressed with red lava rock, on an antique leather book embossed 1823, with autumn leaves nearby

notes: Cosmetic scissors are realistically the only tool I could use to prune and defoliate this little fellow. 3 years after starting the project, the Tricho is forming some nice pads (for a shito bonsai) and moving more and more towards an accentuated slanting position, hinting at a semi-cascade.
alt: Mini desert rose with bristly foliage in a turquoise-rimmed textured pot, outdoors on a huge dried leaf beside bonsai shears

notes: The start of a lovely mame-size desert project. The 'tree' was already about 4 years old at this stage, but it has a veeeery slow growth. Still ugly at the time and not showing much potential, yet in a couple of years it exceeded my expectations.
alt: Pygmy porkbush in a terracotta pot lit by warm side light, an old handwritten manuscript behind and a feather in the foreground

notes: A jungle in a pot! The disheveled look of my old Ficus after spending a season in a small balcony greenhouse, right after a full defoliation and before major pruning. The first aerial roots started showing up as well. A while before I planted some Ficus pumila vines at the base for accent, but they've conquered the canopy in the lush humidity of the greenhouse. The tree was about 14 years old and 11 years in training here. Looks like I forgot to turn the HDR setting off when I took the shot, but it gives it a dramatic effect; I kind of like it.
alt: Freshly defoliated weeping fig above a mossy mound, ringed by its own cut leaves scattered on a wooden board

notes: First bloom of a Murraya germinated from seed, less than 2 years old at the time. Still in the collection, now transitioning from a semi-cascade to a full cascade style. It has bloomed every winter since, and the fragrance is incredible - this tiny thing can fill up a whole room.
alt: Young orange jasmine with white blossoms cascading from a tall white mug beside a cast-iron teapot, casting sharp shadows on the wall

notes: At about 4 years old, the trunk was already gaining character and bending towards a slanting, possibly semi-cascade position. Gave it a better, rusty-crackled pot in 2022, courtesy of Sergey Bogopolski (Сергей Богопольский), Chernivtsi.
alt: Mini desert rose in a red crackle-glaze pot on stacked antique books, next to a small animal skull and a striped feather

notes: Great species for extra-mini bonsai (mame, shito and keshitsubo), not-so-great for bonsai design. It's very drought-tolerant and can thrive with minute quantities of soil and the leaves are quite small. But! It has a bad habit of winter dieback (including mature branches) and tends to form a caudex, quite hard to shape as a bonsai trunk. Still hangs around in my collection, maybe I'll get somewhere with it.
alt: Ice plant with silvery leaves and a swollen storage root in a cracked white bowl on a marbled book, a framed old map behind

notes: First bloom after its first year of training. The small pot and full sun on a southern windowsill worked like a charm. It's starting to develop some ramification which will become the basis of the future crown, and leans towards a slanting position.
alt: Mini desert rose blooming with a single magenta flower, in a cream bowl resting on a green French book in low warm light

notes: Still blooming every summer, still reaching for the sun, the old Bouga at peak growth. I lost it in 2024. Thinking back, I can't believe how many years it managed to survive in full-indoor conditions.
alt: Overhead view of the paperflower sending up long red-flowering shoots from a pot ringed in black, beside a brass pump sprayer on a red book

notes: About 5 years old at the time, it was getting in shape towards a twisted, gnarled, semi-cascade. It never showed too much promise and had weak growth most of the time, but it bloomed every summer. I lost it in 2024. Thinking back, I can't believe how many years it managed to survive in full-indoor conditions.
alt: Paperflower with deep pink bracts arching from a carved coconut-shell pot against a black background

notes: Also tried my hand with Crassulas while experimenting with succulents. This could have become an interesting savannah-style, mame-size mini bonsai, but nature had other plans and I lost it (and most other Crassulas) to either leaf burning, root rot or both. Not great, not terrible.
alt: Slender jade tree with an S-curved trunk in a cracked cream pot with a rock, beside half a lemon and basil leaves on a cutting board

notes: Also tried my hand with Crassulas while experimenting with succulents. This could have become an interesting savannah-style, mame-size mini bonsai, but nature had other plans and I lost it (and most other Crassulas) to either leaf burning, root rot or both. Not great, not terrible.
alt: Compact jade tree with red-tipped leaves in a dark blue glazed pot dressed with rocks, staged with a vintage matchbox and a smoking pipe

notes: The start of a shito-sized project with a tiny arid species (coin added for size comparison). Due to a lack of any outdoor setting and indoor space, I started experimenting more and more with woody succulents as mini-bonsai around 2017-2020. Some projects were dead-ends from the start, while others still grow well & happy. My soft spot for mini-bonsai and desert plants remained.
alt: Young mini desert rose starts sprouting around a rock in a cracked pale-blue bowl, set on a thick leather-bound tome

notes: First repotting of the old Ficus to a proper bonsai pot, courtesy of Zsolt Demeter (Chimi Bonsai Pots). The tree was already about 11 years old here and the new crown was starting to take shape. Still very weak trunks at the time.
alt: Weeping fig with dense glossy foliage and roots gripping pebbles in a green oval pot, lit dramatically against black

notes: I started this back in 2015 from a very small cutting. It never showed too much promise and had weak growth most of the time, but it bloomed every summer. I lost it in 2024. Thinking back, I can't believe how many years it managed to survive in full-indoor conditions.
alt: Two-frame collage of the paperflower’s first vivid magenta blooms on a zigzag branch, in a dark bowl against a black background

notes: Same old Ficus, here after a repot and complete structural restart (only main branches kept).
alt: Weeping fig in a blue rectangular glazed pot with ivy at its base, on a stack of newspaper-wrapped books next to a small potted succulent

notes: One of my earliest 'bonsai' attempts that still hangs around in my collection. Shot taken right after the initial 'styling', from a typical supermarket bush. Almost died twice, got several complete makeovers, the trunks and lower main branches are all that remain from that time. Can't wait to show how the tree evolved after 14+ years.
alt: The weeping fig as a young plant, a single thin trunk in a plain white bowl against a black background

notes: My very first attempt at 'bonsai', collected during spring, 2009. Here pictured a year later in its wooden training pot. The wiring work is, eh... less than ideal. :) I planted it back to nature 1 or 2 years later, realising it's never going to become a bonsai. The original photo was taken by a 3.2 Mpx camera of a Sony Ericsson K800i, so I had to sharpen it a bit using Gemini.
alt: Field elm with twisting twin trunks and serrated leaves, planted in a rustic wooden box with moss and white gravel
